description: A template is a piece of pre-built page structure that someone (often a developer or site admin) has saved separately. When you apply a template to a page, the page gets the template's structure overlaid: some blocks are fixed and can't be edited, some can be edited but not moved, and some are open slots where you fill in your own blocks.

parent.description: This guide is for content editors using a Hydra-powered site. It covers how to use the editor — how to select things, edit text, add and move blocks, work with containers and templates — without assuming you know how the site was built.
parent.title: Editor Guide

title: Templates and layouts

p-1.plaintext: A **template** is a piece of pre-built page structure that someone (often a developer or site admin) has saved separately. When you apply a template to a page, the page gets the template's structure overlaid: some blocks are fixed and can't be edited, some can be edited but not moved, and some are open slots where you fill in your own blocks.

children.text: is a piece of pre-built page structure that someone (often a developer or site admin) has saved separately. When you apply a template to a page, the page gets the template's structure overlaid: some blocks are fixed and can't be edited, some can be edited but not moved, and some are open slots where you fill in your own blocks.

p-2.plaintext: Templates let editors reuse a layout consistently across many pages without copy-pasting structure each time. Two flavours:

children.text: Templates let editors reuse a layout consistently across many pages without copy-pasting structure each time. Two flavours:

ul-3.plaintext: Block-level templates — a snippet you insert as a block (e.g. a "feature row" snippet you drop into the middle of a page). Configured per field as allowedTemplates; appears in the BlockChooser's Templates group. Page-level layouts — a full layout the whole page (or a region) is rendered through (e.g. an "article layout" with a fixed header / sidebar / footer). Configured as allowedLayouts; appears in the Layout dropdown.

p-4.plaintext: The two share the same merge rules; the difference is just where they're applied.

children.text: The two share the same merge rules; the difference is just where they're applied.

p-6.plaintext: When a template is applied to a page, blocks fall into three categories:

children.text: When a template is applied to a page, blocks fall into three categories:

p-8.plaintext: Shown with a **lock icon** in the sidebar block list. You can't:

children.text: in the sidebar block list. You can't:

ul-9.plaintext: Edit the text/media inside it. Move it (no drag handle in the Quanta toolbar). Delete it.

children.text: Move it (no drag handle in the Quanta toolbar).

p-10.plaintext: Typical use: branded headers, footers, legal disclaimers — content the template author wants identical across every page.

children.text: Typical use: branded headers, footers, legal disclaimers — content the template author wants identical across every page.

p-12.plaintext: Shown without a lock but without a drag handle. You can:

children.text: Shown without a lock but without a drag handle. You can:

ul-13.plaintext: Edit text, media, links inside it. Change its block-level settings.

children.text: Edit text, media, links inside it.

ul-15.plaintext: Move it to a different position. Delete it.

children.text: Move it to a different position.

p-16.plaintext: Typical use: a "callout" block in the middle of a layout — every page has one, but the content varies.

children.text: Typical use: a "callout" block in the middle of a layout — every page has one, but the content varies.

p-18.plaintext: Regular blocks where you can do anything — add, edit, move, delete. The template marks regions as slots (with a `slotId`) and your existing content is placed into the matching slots when the template merges.

children.text: Regular blocks where you can do anything — add, edit, move, delete. The template marks regions as slots (with a
) and your existing content is placed into the matching slots when the template merges.

img-19.alt: A page with a snippet template applied. The "Snippet Header" block is selected, rendered in muted style as a locked block. The sidebar shows the template's settings (Template Name, Save Location).

p-21.plaintext: You **can't** insert a new block between two adjacent fixed/readonly template blocks — the "+" button is hidden in those positions and DnD is rejected. This is intentional: the template author put those fixed blocks side-by-side on purpose, and the editor inserting between them would break the layout's intent.

children.text: insert a new block between two adjacent fixed/readonly template blocks — the "+" button is hidden in those positions and DnD is rejected. This is intentional: the template author put those fixed blocks side-by-side on purpose, and the editor inserting between them would break the layout's intent.

p-22.plaintext: If you need to add content there, you may need to switch to a different layout (one whose structure has a slot in that position) or talk to whoever maintains the templates.

children.text: If you need to add content there, you may need to switch to a different layout (one whose structure has a slot in that position) or talk to whoever maintains the templates.

p-24.plaintext: When `allowedLayouts` is configured for a page (or a region), the sidebar shows a **Layout** dropdown. Pick a different layout and:

ol-25.plaintext: The new layout's structure replaces the old one. Your existing content is redistributed into the new layout's slots based on slotId:

children.text: The new layout's structure replaces the old one.
into the new layout's slots based on

p-26.plaintext: - Content tagged with a slot name is placed into the matching slot in the new layout.    - Content with no slot tag falls into the `"default"` slot if the new layout has one; otherwise into the bottom or top slot, or is dropped.    - Fixed blocks with the same `slotId` get their editable content carried over (text, media); their structural settings come from the new layout.

children.text: - Content tagged with a slot name is placed into the matching slot in the new layout.    - Content with no slot tag falls into the
slot if the new layout has one; otherwise into the bottom or top slot, or is dropped.    - Fixed blocks with the same
get their editable content carried over (text, media); their structural settings come from the new layout.

p-27.plaintext: The point of `slotId` is that two layouts can share the same set of region names — switch between them and your content lands in the right places automatically.

children.text: is that two layouts can share the same set of region names — switch between them and your content lands in the right places automatically.

p-29.plaintext: Editing inside a template block (one that came from the merged template) goes through a special **template edit mode**. While in template edit mode:

children.text: Editing inside a template block (one that came from the merged template) goes through a special

ul-30.plaintext: Blocks inside the template instance become editable, including those marked readonly. Blocks outside the template instance become locked.

children.text: Blocks inside the template instance become editable, including those marked readonly.
Blocks outside the template instance become locked.

p-31.plaintext: This lets you edit the template's *definition* without leaving the page — the changes propagate back to the saved template, so any other page using it picks up the change. Switch out of template edit mode (typically a toolbar action labelled "Edit template" / "Done") to go back to editing this page's content.

children.text: This lets you edit the template's

without leaving the page — the changes propagate back to the saved template, so any other page using it picks up the change. Switch out of template edit mode (typically a toolbar action labelled "Edit template" / "Done") to go back to editing this page's content.

p-33.plaintext: When a template is applied, the template's blocks are grouped under a single virtual entry in the sidebar block list — you'll see "Template: Article Layout" rather than each fixed/readonly block as a separate row. Expand it to see the structure inside.

children.text: When a template is applied, the template's blocks are grouped under a single virtual entry in the sidebar block list — you'll see "Template: Article Layout" rather than each fixed/readonly block as a separate row. Expand it to see the structure inside.

h-34.plaintext: Inserting a template as a block

children.text: Inserting a template as a block

p-35.plaintext: When `allowedTemplates` is configured on a field, the BlockChooser's Templates group shows the list. Pick one and the template's content is inserted at the current position as a block (just like adding any other block). Editor-side this is the simpler case — once inserted, the template's blocks behave the same as if a layout had placed them (lock icons, fixed-but-editable, slots).
